item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If a subject 18 and older brings a second subject within, the first subject will enter successfully, but the second subject will be teleported outside of SCP-2341 and the door will close.

Just wondering, how does this work? Are they just jarringly thrown out the moment they step in, or do they open the door to find nothing?

item.title: Re: New Image
item.content:encoded: Noting that the first image comes from here and is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry to find you're not around anymore, but here's a reading anyway. :C

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I'm not sure what I'm supposed to "get" about the ending. I don't think the link between the gray entity, the note, and the oil can, is strong enough to make the ending clear.

Other than that, there are some sections that I think need a re-write:

SCP-2341-1 instances will begin for sleep after activities have been completed or have grown tired. Toiletries such as facial cream or retainers are present for the subject to use. Afterwards, once the subject has entered their room, they will enter sleep and will be teleported to the front door of SCP-2341.

I would change it to something like:

Instances of SCP-2341-1 will prepare to sleep once they have completed their activities. Appropriate toiletries, such as facial cream or retainers, will appear for the subject to use during said preparation. Afterward, the subject will enter their bedroom and lay down. Upon falling asleep, they will be teleported to the front door of SCOP-2341 unharmed.

Also:

SCP-2341’s anomalous properties activate only when a human subject older than 18 years of age opens the front door. Only one subject at a time can enter, but cannot leave until the anomalous events have concluded.

Should be:

SCP-2341's anomalous properties only activate when the front door is opened by someone 18 years or older. Only one subject can enter at a time. They cannot leave until the anomolous events have concluded.

There are a few other places that are similarly *off*, but I'd rather not type them all out. The formatting is also a little too messy. Maybe you could try adding this part:

Addendum 2341.2: Drone exploration
On █/█/2015, Researcher Andrew proposed sending a drone into the interior of SCP-2341 while the front door of SCP-2341 is open in an attempt to see the original interior of SCP-2341. The proposal has been authorized.

…inside, and to the top of, the gray text box below it. Maybe you can put the picture within, and at the bottom of, that text box as well. Hard to say if that will look good without trying it.
_

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seeing as Amuness is not currently available, at this point I assume indefinitely, I've gone through and done some touching up as the co-author.

Edit: I'm also assuming that custodianship falls to me since the other co-author has left. If I'm wrong, someone please correct me on that.

item.title: Staff Post: Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 23 of this article has been reverted, as it was made with the permission of a user who could not actually give said permission.

item.title: SCP-2341 in IRL!
item.content:encoded:
Guys! Kola found the news story for SCP-2341! Read it up yo!

I mean it's about water levels rising but it's still interesting!

item.title: Re: New Image
item.content:encoded:
You have a very nice kitchen, Amuness!

(Honestly, I'd rather stay with the one picture of the exterior of the house, and let the readers imagine whatever is Inside. But you're the boss!)

item.title: Re: Multiple homes
item.content:encoded: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-1289218/scp-2341#post-2371951

item.title: Multiple homes
item.content:encoded: What if, like me, the subject entering the house had lived in more than one house before they turned 18?

item.title: New Image
item.content:encoded: Hey guys! Just added a new image to SCP-2341. It was taken by me, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(from Kalinin's post)

There is no relateability here for the reader.

As soon as I read about the anomalous effects of the skip, I went 'YEAH! To talk to my father again!' and remained flustered for the rest of my reading.
Which is not why I upvoted immediately.
Well, yeah, maybe it is. A bit.

Frankly, me myself I, I don't care if it follows TOO MUCH (?) the rules of SCP-writing. I read, I get grabbed, I think about it, I don't get all the answers but most of the time I like it that way, good writing, good idea, good picture. That's all it takes for me to like and upvote this SCP.

I do agree that some points are not covered:

+ show block
– hide block

It has been noted that references to an entity by the name of “Peter” have been present in various SCP-2341-1 conversations.

I'd like to see one instance, or a few instances of it. How does it come into the conversation? Is it a reference in passing or something more important?

For people who loved and miss their family, isn't there a risk of addiction to visiting this SCP? Yeah, I would be one of the addicted… My father's conversations were VERY NICE!!!! And the house in Montjay? Lemme check - yeah, that's where I lived the longest before 18. I would be constantly sneaking into that SCP!!!!!!

Outside observation through the windows will be blocked by the curtains inside of SCP-2341

What prevents the subject from moving the curtains to look outside? Better have blocked shutters or something, OR, alternatively, have the exterior correspond to the interior for the duration of the anomaly! (I could see the old cherry tree and beautiful garden outside the house in Montjay)

Is it really 'Little Thatcher Island'? Google Maps shows it as 'Little Thatch Island'… (of course I had to check!!!!!)

Electric fence so close to all this water… mmmmh… Isn't there a better way to contain a sandbank?

What happens if the subject's family was extremely conflictual?

I also like Snowglobe's ideas, especially the second one re: homeless D-class.

… Actually, I'm torn: I don't know if I want to advise you to do something more with this SCP because it has a lot of potential, OR if I find it perfect as is (with just a few minor corrections, see above) and PLEASE DO NOT TOUCH IT.
Anyway. Good job, Amuness and Fae!

<EC strolls away, daydreaming about Daddy and Montjay>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I like the base idea, I feel it needs to be polished. Also, the note at the end is too 'in your face' and feels forced, I can't feel someone really wrote that for one specific person, feels like it was written to be found and give context, specifically.

Other than that I have a question, since I'm honestly not good with details in this weird language that's English.
"identical to the home that the subject had lived for the first 18 years of life."
Isn't that missing an 'in' or 'on'?
Lived on, lived in?
Or that does make sense in English and is one of those rules I stil haven't learnt?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I'm kinda proud of those ideas, and I'm glad you like them too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like these suggestions. Thanks! Though, I think the article is at a good ranking as of right now, but if I do have to rewrite it in the future then I'll use those suggestions. That would be a lot of feels right there as LurkD said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like others have said, it's interesting about until the 'grey humanoid' appears. What the house does is interesting, and 'Peter' manifesting in the house is interesting, but after that it feels weaker, largely due to being kind of typical for an scp.

If I may, I'd like to suggest that the cause be - instead of the child - a would-be parent who was really excited to have children, but somehow never did, and the scp creates the life they pictured for themselves.

I'd also like to suggest that the foundation would test this scp with a d-class who was homeless for the first 18 years of their life, and possibly one who has amnesia that includes the first 18 years of their life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I have to underline this part because this is becoming really common:

Subjects that had moved during this time period will enter the home that they lived in the most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"When entering SCP-2341, the interior will reshape itself to look identical to the home that the subject had lived for the first 18 years of life."

What's it do when a person has moved around a lot as a pre-18-year-old? That's a really common circumstance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can hit the edit button on your first comment ya know instead of making another one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: persuaded to downvote. I hadn't put my finger on what was bugging me before, but Kalinin said it better than I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ominous note is ominous. i think it might be better if scp-2341-2 and the note were dropped, and the rest were kept… or there was a lot more. not satisfied. neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm…I see. Thanks for the tagging.

EDIT: I added the co-authored tag since Fae collaborated with me on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like you've barely scratched the surface with this one. There is potential for a lot feels here but it's not pushed far enough. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I'm still not getting anything out of this. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this whole post. I don't think it's too much to ask that each new piece incorporates some narrative device or element or idea that hasn't proven itself in some other piece; this article treads no new ground and does not do a great job of treading the old one either.

It's a waste of a cool picture too, I might add. Not only in the sense that the article isn't good, but in that none of the cool aspects are really incorporated well into the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason I am reminded of an episode of Johnny Bravo.
It's the babysitter episode. With the, well, realitybending child.

The vibe I get off of this is that this Peter who is mentioned is the bender -or at least something with a supernatural ability- that has created for itself a 'perfect family home environment- and has been using powers to enforce this on everyone.
My idea is that the people trapped in this situation know very well what's going on and how they're being forced to act and in a few sparse moments of having free will thought up a way to get out, and make sure this never happens again.

Eventually this plan culminated in setting everything and everyone on fire. I figure the entity was spared because of its powers shielding it from getting crisped.
Now, either it was imprinted on one (or both) of the now charred bodies and can't get away from this spot now or its mind 'broke' and it's stuck crying in place.

The anomalous effect could then just be either it trying to recreate that family environment again with a new 'host' to imprint on and leave the house with, or an automatic effect of its mind trying to repair the traumatic experience.
The latter feels like it's closer to what is going on due to the slow, but increasing, appearances of the non-family member entity during the events.

I could be fully and completely off here. But that's what I take from it and it makes me fear for what might happen if 'Peter' does get out… Might be it'll start looking for 'Fitzgerald'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding all of this, I also felt that the logistics of some of the scips actions were a bit weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah Never mind for real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry you didn't enjoy it :C

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My main issues

1. A lot of the language feels jumbled to me.

SCP-2341-1 instances that resemble deceased family members are aware of the original counterpart's current condition, but will not treat their appearance as an irregular occurrence.

That's an incredibly awkward sentence to my eye. There's a lot of that.

2. Footnotes. All that should be in the main documentation I think.

3. Why should Level 2 personnel have any kind of say over who can visit this thing? Even 3 of them. Also, you use one Doctor's surname only, and the following uses full name.

4. Redacting the dates makes no sense. It seems like it was done just because SCPs have redactions.

Sorry. I like the concept, however I feel the execution needs some work.

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure. Gonna stay neutral with this one. I think the connection between the anomaly and the grey dude needs to be strengthened and given additional 'creep' factor. I think this connection could lie in what the guy actually did. Revealing his crime and giving it a connection between the anomaly and him might be interesting.
Some minor improvements are needed, other than that, good article and interesting premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If there's spontaneous anomalous activity, shouldn't it be Euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This falls squarely in the "just good enough by general standards to stick around" category. I would hope we've reached a point in our evolution that "just good enough" is not something that we should be content with.

Just going to say that this is precisely why I'm rewriting a lot of my early contributions to the wiki, and that this is a sentiment I fully agree with.

As far as the piece itself is concerned, I'm, like, super sorry I didn't get around to replying to the PM asking for critique, and I'm not sure how I want to vote. On the one hand, I was creeped out by 2341-2 for a little bit. On the other, it's kind of a generic creepy (see the Zach Callahan comparisons). More than that, the actual, main anomaly is not one that does it for me. A weird, gray humanoid and two charred corpses making you think you're with your own family doesn't cut it for me. I don't see the connection between the two things, the family thing and the sad spoopy humanoid. If the main anomaly were more connected to the source, I'd be more inclined to upvote, and vague similarity to 2110 isn't helping my opinion.

For now, I'm abstaining from voting. I may change my mind at a later time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This falls squarely in the "just good enough by general standards to stick around" category. I would hope we've reached a point in our evolution that "just good enough" is not something that we should be content with.

+ My general issues
- Hide text
  • I'm becoming wary now whenever I see visual fuckery, because more often than not it's not really demanded by the narrative and is usually present to distract from the writing itself. I'm not saying that people shouldn't use stuff like the Foundation logo watermarks or different formatting for addenda, but it seems a little slick for an organization like what the Foundation is for most writers. I don't think an organization dedicated to secrecy is going to generally plaster official logos all over their reports. Visual presentation should be pushed forward (I'm reminded of McTiriss' efforts in this regard), but not at the expense of narrative or as a cheap means of garnering upvotes. I don't really see the reason it's here, in this case.
  • Once again, we have a notion among some reviewers that because they don't get what's going on, they must be failing to grasp some key element of the story and give the writer the benefit of the doubt. And again, I must point out that the writer's job is to tell the story. The writer in this case does not even know the story, and it's evident in the writing. This doesn't mean having to spell things out directly all the time (god forbid) or drawing a point-by-point map for the reader, but there should be something to follow. Frankly, throwing things out just to be mysterious for no apparent reason is bullshit, in the truest sense of the word.
+ My particular issues
- Hide text
  • The execution is generally okay up until the last addendum. This is the first time I've seen a "note at the end" that is both excessively convenient (as all of them inevitably are) while simultaneously having zero meaningful content. The weird lined paper formatting was distracting; if this in-universe, I would think a picture of the note and a transcription would make more sense. Out-of-universe, there's no reason for it to be formatted like that. And the author bolded a handwritten note?
  • People have brought up Zachary Callahan already, but really, if this article treads too closely to anything, it's SCP-2110. I generally don't mind if the same subject material gets covered from multiple angles, but this hews way too closely in setup and execution to be considered truly independent of that article's influence, in my opinion. And in an article that lacks meat or characterization like this one does, that really becomes a problem in terms of its value on the wiki.
  • This may not be a compulsion effect in name, but it suffers from the same problem. There is no relateability here for the reader. No point of view to consider this from. Everyone who gets put in this house, despite it being tailored in a deeply personal manner to them, gets to do the exact same routine, and react the exact same way to it. If it's not a compulsion effect, this actually makes even less sense, since instead of reacting with shock (and in a lot of cases horror given family histories) at the sudden reappearance of childhood settings and dead relations, the subject just sort of go along with some stereotypical family activities. Nevermind if the person actually did these things at any point. People act like robots in this piece, and thus it neither rings true nor does it interest me at all.
  • The spooky disembodied voices, humanoid monster thing and corpses and whatever all feel like extremely contrived horror tropes that are being hammered into the piece inexpertly, and for no apparent reason. Also, as has been mentioned before, the last note is extremely narmy.

There are cases where there are articles that are bogged down by a by-the-numbers execution, the interesting core of an idea obscured by things like too much attention being paid to self-satisfying format screws or other extraneous elements. This article, however, is really nothing more than a by-the-numbers execution in its entirety. It's essentially a decorated stage with some props and lighting, and nobody's on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can barely get this thing to do totally normal stuff reliably, and I've got "very high" karma. It doesn't actually mean anything except that you either have a lot of free time or been around wikidot since dirt and aren't shy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would disagree that it's totally useless. It does indicate a certain level of activity and experience using wikidot. It doesn't necessarily mean they are more or less qualified to comment on a draft, or grammar, or anything of that nature. But it does mean that they've been around and presumably seen some stuff, and commented or created pages of their own, albeit not necessarily on this wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair warning, karma means NOTHING. Zero. Zip. Nada.

It means you've posted some things on wikidot wikis. That and $3 will get you a coffee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of dull. Nothing really that kept me engaged throughout. Not poorly written, but not executed well enough to earn my upvote.

Specifically, this line—

we did what we had to do. we had to stop him. may god forgive us for what we did. do not let him out.

—is very cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
have low karma

Fair warning, karma means NOTHING. Zero. Zip. Nada. Doesn't matter what position your karma is in: it depends on the user.

Also I'm glad you like it! Thanks for the grammar issue, I'll edit it in the morning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-2341's interior change, during its anomalous effects"

I can't tell if "during its anomalous effects" makes sense or not, but that's it for grammar. I like how a back story is hinted at but not given away, and overall I think it's a well written scp. My opinion probably doesn't matter though because I have low karma, but ta gotta start somewhere :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's true of every article posted on this site. Isn't it? You can always a) delete your own works and b) post rewrites of deleted works (in accordance with the rewrite policy).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
non-experimental upload

An experimental upload is testing to see if the community likes it or not. I'm just testing if some people will like or if some will not. If the community likes it, then fuck ya If not then well Fae and I will work on it some more to get it perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh…OK then! After all, this is an experimental upload. I'm not sure if this is actually gonna be on here for long. However, (as I said in my author post) if this gets near deletion, then remove it and refine it.

I'm not sure I understand what that means (or how that differs from a non-experimental upload). I thought you meant there's some experimental novel elements to this, but if there are I'm missing them.

More to it huh? Well, I do have something in mind…maybe.

A hint would be fine. o_O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh…OK then! After all, this is an experimental upload. I'm not sure if this is actually gonna be on here for long. However, (as I said in my author post) if this gets near deletion, then remove it and refine it.

More to it huh? Well, I do have something in mind…maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's ok. I have no idea what your missing but I just hope you enjoyed it a little :C

I'm missing whatever is actually going on here. All I'm getting is that somebody (probably named Peter) was burned up in the house by somebody named Susan and now they haunt the place by projecting an illusionary and familiar home environment on anybody who enters. I'm hoping there's more to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what if someone lived in more than one home before they were 18? What about kids moving between foster families?

It said that in the article which ever housed they lived in the most is the one SCP-2341 changes into.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the story behind this, though I'll say that I had flashes of Zachary Callahan with the -2. That said, the thing keeping me from an upvote: what if someone lived in more than one home before they were 18? What about kids moving between foster families?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: Wow, I missed a line. Oops.

EDIT 2: This seems to touch on familiar subjects, and this doesn't particularly engage me or interest me the whole way through. The inclusion of the grey humanoid brings it down a bit, because it's just so typical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's ok. I have no idea what your missing but I just hope you enjoyed it a little :C

I just want to people to be entertained by this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture is interesting, and the piece is well written, but I'm not sure I'm putting the clues together correctly. As it is it seems like a pretty ordinary ghost story. I'm sure I'm missing something. So currently no-voting.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is a collaboration SCP between me (Amuness Creeps) and DreamwalkerFae.

Project Time: 41 days

Image credits: Credit goes to the Visual Records for the image of SCP-2341. Credit goes to djkaktus for the SCP logo on the timeline chart Credit goes to LurkD for providing the notebook paper image on Addendum 2341.3. I created the site48 logo on the time chart

Credits for critique: Professor Will, Dr Hysteria, WoggleBug, Soundchaser, TwistedGears and various people from site19 (i.e. minmin)

Post: For right now, this is an experimental upload of the SCP to see if it survives or not. Fae and I agreed to do this. If it falls into the negative range or is at 1 to 10 up votes, then the article will get deleted and it will be significantly edited. However, if this SCP goes past 10 up votes and to the twenties range, then that’s great!

When 2 days have past or when staff put in tags, then I will declare this as a successful SCP. I really like short SCPs as they’re easy to read and don’t really need to go into full detail of things. Short ones are the best as they’re quick and easy to read with a story behind it :]

Crossing my fingers that this goes well.

30 upvotes!? MY LIFE IS COMPLETE!! (Well not really) >_>

OK 44 up votes is amazing thank you
